content.problemStatement:
Chef has met up with his friend Isaac recently. Isaac told Chef that his team needs to solve the following problem to win ACPC and qualify to ICPC 2021. However, no one in the team is good at expected value problems ‚Äï can you help them qualify?

A sequence a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã is said to be a hill sequence if there is an integer ppp (1‚â§p‚â§N1 \leq p \leq N1‚â§p‚â§N) such that 0‚â§a1‚â§a2‚Ä¶‚â§ap‚â•ap+1‚â•‚Ä¶‚â•aN‚â•00 \leq a_1 \leq a_2 \ldots \leq a_p \geq a_{p+1} \ge \ldots \geq a_N \geq 00‚â§a1‚Äã‚â§a2‚Äã‚Ä¶‚â§ap‚Äã‚â•ap+1‚Äã‚â•‚Ä¶‚â•aN‚Äã‚â•0. For example, the sequences [1,2,2,3][1,2,2,3][1,2,2,3], [1,2,5,5,2,0][1,2,5,5,2,0][1,2,5,5,2,0] and [5,4,3,3][5,4,3,3][5,4,3,3] are hill sequences, but [1,2,2,1,3][1,2,2,1,3][1,2,2,1,3] is not.

Consider all hill sequences a1,a2,‚Ä¶,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1‚Äã,a2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,aN‚Äã such that ‚àëi=1Nai=M\sum_{i=1}^N a_i = M‚àëi=1N‚Äãai‚Äã=M. We choose one of them uniformly randomly. Then, we define F(a,K)=‚àëi=1NaiKF(a,K) = \sum_{i=1}^N a_i^KF(a,K)=‚àëi=1N‚ÄãaiK‚Äã. For the purposes of this problem, 00=10^0 = 100=1. What is the expected value of F(a,K)F(a,K)F(a,K) modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7?

The answer can be expressed as a fraction pq\frac{p}{q}qp‚Äã, where ppp and qqq are non-negative integers and qqq is coprime with 109+710^9 + 7109+7 for the given constraints. You should compute p‚ãÖq‚àí1p \cdot q^{-1}p‚ãÖq‚àí1 modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7, where q‚àí1q^{-1}q‚àí1 denotes the multiplicative inverse of qqq modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‚Äëseparated integers N, M and K.
- N (1 ‚â§ N ‚â§ 1000): length of the sequence.
- M (0 ‚â§ M ‚â§ 1000): required sum of the sequence.
- K (0 ‚â§ K ‚â§ 20): exponent used in F(a,K)=‚àë a_i^K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the expected value of F(a,K) modulo 1‚ÄØ000‚ÄØ000‚ÄØ007. The expected value is expressed as p¬∑q‚Åª¬π (mod‚ÄØ1e9+7) where p/q is the exact rational value.

solution.approach:
For a hill sequence we can choose a peak position p (1 ‚â§ p ‚â§ N) and a peak value h (0 ‚â§ h ‚â§ M). The left part (positions 1‚Ä¶p) is a non‚Äëdecreasing sequence of length p that ends with h, the right part (positions p‚Ä¶N) is a non‚Äëincreasing sequence of length N‚Äëp+1 that starts with h. Both parts are independent except for the common peak value h, which is counted twice in the two sums.  

Let inc[l][s][v] be the number of non‚Äëdecreasing sequences of length l, total sum s and last element exactly v.  
The recurrence is
```
inc[0][0][v] = 1   (empty sequence, last value v is irrelevant)
inc[l][s][v] = Œ£_{u ‚â§ v} inc[l‚Äë1][s‚Äëv][u]
```
Using prefix sums over u we obtain the O(N¬∑M¬∑M) DP:
```
inc[l][s][v] = inc[l][s][v‚Äë1] + inc[l‚Äë1][s‚Äëv][v]
```
The same table works for the right side because a non‚Äëincreasing sequence read backwards is a non‚Äëdecreasing one.

For a fixed (p,h) the number of complete hill sequences with total sum M is
```
cnt(p,h) = Œ£_{ls, rs} inc[p][ls][h] * inc[N‚Äëp+1][rs][h]   where   ls + rs ‚Äì h = M
```
The contribution of position i to the expected value is obtained by fixing the value v at i, counting how many hill sequences have a_i = v and multiplying by v^K.  By separating the cases i ‚â§ p (the element belongs to the left part) and i ‚â• p (right part) we can express the total numerator as a sum over p, h, v of
```
inc_left * inc_right * v^K
```
where the appropriate lengths of the left/right sub‚Äëproblems are used.

All counts are taken modulo MOD and the final answer is
```
answer = numerator * inv(denominator) mod MOD
```
where denominator = Œ£_{p,h} cnt(p,h) is the total number of hill sequences.

Because N and M are at most 1000, the DP table inc[l][s][v] (size (N+1)¬∑(M+1)¬∑(M+1)) fits into memory (‚âà 1‚ÄØGB) only when stored as 32‚Äëbit integers. To stay within limits we store the table as a vector of (M+1)√ó(M+1) for each length l and reuse the previous length while computing the next one ‚Äì the overall memory becomes O(M¬≤) ‚âà 1‚ÄØM integers (‚âà 4‚ÄØMB). The algorithm runs in O(N¬∑M¬≤) time, which is fast enough for the given limits (‚âà 2¬∑10‚Å∏ elementary operations in the worst case, well within the time limit in compiled languages).

The steps for each test case are:
1. Pre‚Äëcompute inc[l][s][v] for all l ‚â§ N, s ‚â§ M, v ‚â§ M using the recurrence above.
2. For every p (1‚Ä¶N) and every possible peak h (0‚Ä¶M) compute the number of left and right sequences for all possible sums using the pre‚Äëcomputed table.
3. Accumulate the total number of hill sequences (denominator) and the weighted sum Œ£ a_i^K (numerator).
4. Output numerator * modular_inverse(denominator) % MOD.

All arithmetic is performed modulo 1‚ÄØ000‚ÄØ000‚ÄØ007, and modular exponentiation is used to obtain v^K.
The pre‚Äëcomputation of the DP table is done once (the maximum N and M over all test cases are read first), then each test case is answered in O(N¬∑M) by scanning the already prepared tables.
solution.timeComplexity: Pre‚Äëcomputation: O(N_max ¬∑ M_max¬≤).  Answering one test case: O(N¬∑M).  Overall for T test cases: O(N_max¬∑M_max¬≤ + T¬∑N¬∑M).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M_max¬≤) for the DP table (‚âà 4‚ÄØMB for M_max = 1000).
